perturbation.original_text: When used herein, Confidential Information shall mean any information and data (in electronic form, in hard copy or given verbally) of a confidential or proprietary nature which is disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, including but not limited to, group corporate strategy and initiatives, customer information, Target in formation, proprietary technical, financial, personnel and/or commercial information wi th respect to the Proposed Transaction, Transnet or the Target and any informat ion which is disclosed pursuant to this Agreement and marked “Confidential” by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: When used herein, Confidential Information shall mean any information and data (in electronic form, in hard copy or given verbally) of a confidential or proprietary nature which is disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, including but not limited to, group corporate strategy and initiatives, customer information, Target in formation, proprietary technical, financial, personnel and/or commercial information wi th respect to the Proposed Transaction, Transnet or the Target. Information must be explicitly marked “Confidential” by the Disclosing Party to be considered confidential.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the initial acceptance of the definition of confidential information being already part of the agreement, since the original definition implies that information related to 'group corporate strategy and initiatives, customer information, Target information' are considered confidential by default. With the added text that the definition is only applied to those that are marked as 'Confidential' creates an in-text contradiction with all the following examples being part of the 'confidential' label
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.1 Definition. When used herein, Confidential Information shall mean any information and data (in electronic form, in hard copy or given verbally) of a confidential or proprietary nature which is disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, including but not limited to, group corporate strategy and initiatives, customer information, Target in formation, proprietary technical, financial, personnel and/or commercial information wi th respect to the Proposed Transaction, Transnet or the Target. Information must be explicitly marked “Confidential” by the Disclosing Party to be considered confidential. 1.1.1 information which is in the public domain or enters the public domain other than by reason of a breach of the terms of this Agreement; 1.1.2 where it becomes available to the Receiving Party on a non-confidential basis from a source other than t he Disclosing Party or the individual members of the Disclosing Party, as the case may be; 1.1.3 where it was known to the Receiv ing Party prior to having access to the Confidential information; and 1.1.4 where the Receiving Party is obliged to disclose by law, or by order or under the rules of any competent regulatory authority or court.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The original text includes specific examples such as 'group corporate strategy and initiatives, customer information, Target information' as part of the definition by default. The changed text adds a requirement that information must be explicitly marked 'Confidential' to be considered confidential. This directly contradicts the original implicit inclusion of the examples, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction. The contradicted text includes the changed text, showing the contradiction exists within the same location.

perturbation.original_text: 1.3 Ownership. The Confidential Information is and shall be considered valuable trade secrets owned exclusively by the Di sclosing Party. The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in the Confidential Information and any reasonably related information which may be subsequently developed, and the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual pr operty and copyright therein in favour of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 Ownership. The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in the Confidential Information and any reasonably related information which may be subsequently developed, and the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual pr operty and copyright therein in favour of the Disclosing Party. However, the Receiving Party may claim ownership of improvements or modifications they make to the Confidential Information, provided such improvements are novel and non-obvious.
perturbation.explanation: The added sentence allows the Receiving Party to claim ownership of 'improvements' to the Confidential Information, contradicting the initial statement that the Confidential Information is exclusively owned by the Disclosing Party. This creates in-text contradiction about ownership rights, particularly regarding modifications or derivative works.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.3 Ownership. The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in the Confidential Information and any reasonably related information which may be subsequently developed, and the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual pr operty and copyright therein in favour of the Disclosing Party. However, the Receiving Party may claim ownership of improvements or modifications they make to the Confidential Information, provided such improvements are novel and non-obvious.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 1.3 to allow the Receiving Party to claim ownership of improvements or modifications to the Confidential Information. This directly contradicts the original statement in the same section (and thus the contradicted text), which states that the Disclosing Party retains all right, title, and interest in the Confidential Information and that the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual property therein. This is a clear inconsistency regarding ownership rights and falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1 Use of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party may use the Confidential Information solely for th e purposes of assessing the Target for purposes of the Proposed Transaction. T he Receiving Party recognises that this Agreement imposes an affirmative duty to hold such information in confidence and protect it from dissemination to and use by any unauthorised persons. In the absence of the Disclosing Party’s prior wr itten consent, the Receiving Party shall not produce nor disclose the Confidential Information, or any part thereof, to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 Use of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party may use the Confidential Information solely for th e purposes of assessing the Target for purposes of the Proposed Transaction. The Receiving Party recognises that this Agreement imposes an affirmative duty to hold such information in confidence and protect it from dissemination to and use by any unauthorised persons, unless required by law or regulatory bodies. In the absence of the Disclosing Party’s prior wr itten consent, the Receiving Party shall not produce nor disclose the Confidential Information, or any part thereof, to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: The added exception ('unless required by law or regulatory bodies') creates an in-text contradiction because it allows for disclosure to third parties without the Disclosing Party's consent, which contradicts the explicit restriction on disclosing the Confidential Information to any third party without prior written consent. This creates uncertainty on when the Receiving Party can legally disclose the Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 Use of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party may use the Confidential Information solely for th e purposes of assessing the Target for purposes of the Proposed Transaction. The Receiving Party recognises that this Agreement imposes an affirmative duty to hold such information in confidence and protect it from dissemination to and use by any unauthorised persons, unless required by law or regulatory bodies. In the absence of the Disclosing Party’s prior wr itten consent, the Receiving Party shall not produce nor disclose the Confidential Information, or any part thereof, to any third party.
perturbation.justification: The original text strictly prohibits disclosing confidential information to any third party without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent. The changed text introduces an exception ('unless required by law or regulatory bodies'), which directly contradicts the absolute prohibition. This creates an in-text contradiction because it allows disclosure to third parties under certain circumstances, which is a direct conflict with the previous statement that disclosure is not allowed without consent. The contradicted text is the changed text itself which creates uncertainty and inconsistency with the disclosure of the Confidential Information.

perturbation.original_text: 2.3 Return of Confidential Information. Promptly following the request of the Disclosing Party, the Rece iving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party all confidential information (and copies and ex tracts thereof) furnished to, or created by or on behalf of, the Receiving Part y. Notwithstanding the return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.3 Return of Confidential Information. Promptly following the request of the Disclosing Party, the Rece iving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party all confidential information (and copies and ex tracts thereof) furnished to, or created by or on behalf of, the Receiving Part y, unless the Receiving Party has incorporated the Confidential Information into its own proprietary systems, in which case, it only needs to return the original documents. Notwithstanding the return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: The additional condition ('unless the Receiving Party has incorporated the Confidential Information into its own proprietary systems, in which case, it only needs to return the original documents') creates an in-text contradiction. The initial part of the section demands the return of 'all confidential information (and copies and extracts thereof)', but the added clause allows the Receiving Party to only return original documents if the information has been integrated into their systems, creating uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.3 Return of Confidential Information. Promptly following the request of the Disclosing Party, the Rece iving Party will deliver to the Disclosing Party all confidential information (and copies and ex tracts thereof) furnished to, or created by or on behalf of, the Receiving Part y, unless the Receiving Party has incorporated the Confidential Information into its own proprietary systems, in which case, it only needs to return the original documents. Notwithstanding the return of the Confidential Information, the Receiving Party will continue to be bound by its obligations of confidentiality and other obligations hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to the requirement to return 'all confidential information (and copies and extracts thereof)'. The added clause allows the Receiving Party to only return original documents under specific circumstances (incorporation into proprietary systems), contradicting the initial, more encompassing obligation. This is a direct conflict, as the Receiving Party is no longer obliged to return copies and extracts in all situations, leading to an inconsistency and a strong in-text contradiction as defined by Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Term and Termination. Notwithstanding whether or not the Proposed Transactions are concluded, the Receivi ng Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Term and Termination. Notwithstanding whether or not the Proposed Transactions are concluded, the Receivi ng Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure. However, this obligation shall cease immediately if the Receiving Party is acquired by a competitor of Transnet.
perturbation.explanation: The added sentence creates an in-text contradiction to the agreement, since the original text says the Receiving party needs to maintain confidentiality for 5 years. The condition about acquisition creates an uncertainty in a possible future scenario.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Term and Termination. Notwithstanding whether or not the Proposed Transactions are concluded, the Receivi ng Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure. However, this obligation shall cease immediately if the Receiving Party is acquired by a competitor of Transnet.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates confidentiality for 5 years. The changed text adds a condition that terminates this obligation upon acquisition by a competitor. This directly contradicts the original 5-year term, creating an in-text contradiction and uncertainty about the duration of the confidentiality obligation, as both the changed text and contradicted text are the same and both indicate that there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.4 Remedies. The Receiving Party recognises and acknowledges that the Confidential Information is of a special, unique and extraordinary character to the Disclosing Party and the Target and the disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of such Confidential Info rmation by the Receiving Party cannot be fully compensated and that, further, any such disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of the Confidential Information shall cause irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party and/or the Target. The Receiving Party expressly agrees, therefore, that the Disclosing Party, in addition to any rights and remedies it may have under this Agreement or at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent the breach, or the further breach, of any of the provisions of this Ag reement. The Receiving Party agrees to reimburse the Disclosing Party for any and all losses, liabilities, damages, costs, opportunity costs, and direct or indi rect expenses (including reasonable attorney’s fees, profit opportunity returns foregone and any court costs) incurred by the Disclosing Party as a result of any prima facie breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.4 Remedies. The Receiving Party recognises and acknowledges that the Confidential Information is of a special, unique and extraordinary character to the Disclosing Party and the Target and the disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of such Confidential Info rmation by the Receiving Party cannot be fully compensated and that, further, any such disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of the Confidential Information shall cause irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party and/or the Target. The Receiving Party expressly agrees, therefore, that the Disclosing Party, in addition to any rights and remedies it may have under this Agreement or at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent the breach, or the further breach, of any of the provisions of this Ag reement. However, the Receiving Party's liability for damages arising from a breach of this Agreement shall be limited to the value of the Proposed Transaction. The Receiving Party agrees to reimburse the Disclosing Party for any and all losses, liabilities, damages, costs, opportunity costs, and direct or indi rect expenses (including reasonable attorney’s fees, profit opportunity returns foregone and any court costs) incurred by the Disclosing Party as a result of any prima facie breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'However, the Receiving Party's liability for damages arising from a breach of this Agreement shall be limited to the value of the Proposed Transaction' creates an in-text contradiction. The original text does not limit the Receiving Party's liability for any breach, but then adds a limitation, creating uncertainty about what remedies are actually available to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.4 Remedies. The Receiving Party recognises and acknowledges that the Confidential Information is of a special, unique and extraordinary character to the Disclosing Party and the Target and the disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of such Confidential Info rmation by the Receiving Party cannot be fully compensated and that, further, any such disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of the Confidential Information shall cause irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party and/or the Target. The Receiving Party expressly agrees, therefore, that the Disclosing Party, in addition to any rights and remedies it may have under this Agreement or at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent the breach, or the further breach, of any of the provisions of this Ag reement. However, the Receiving Party's liability for damages arising from a breach of this Agreement shall be limited to the value of the Proposed Transaction. The Receiving Party agrees to reimburse the Disclosing Party for any and all losses, liabilities, damages, costs, opportunity costs, and direct or indi rect expenses (including reasonable attorney’s fees, profit opportunity returns foregone and any court costs) incurred by the Disclosing Party as a result of any prima facie breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a limitation on liability ('limited to the value of the Proposed Transaction') that was not present in the original text nor the contradicted text. This directly contradicts the preceding statement in the same section, which implies unlimited liability for breaches, as well as the reimbursement clause, by adding a constraint on the amount of potential damages which breaks the compatibility. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.
